examples.title: Test-and-Set Spinlock Implementation
examples.description: Mutual exclusion using TSL instruction in C with inline assembly
examples.explanation: The xchg instruction on x86 atomically exchanges the register value (1) with the memory location. If the old value was 0, lock is acquired. The inline assembly ensures atomicity.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for single acquisition attempt, O(n) worst case with n concurrent contenders

examples.title: Compare-and-Swap Atomic Counter
examples.description: Lock-free atomic counter increment using CAS in Python
examples.code:
import threading

class AtomicCounter:
    def __init__(self, initial=0):
        self._value = initial
        self._lock = threading.Lock()
    
    def compare_and_swap(self, expected, new_value):
        """CAS: update to new_value only if current equals expected"""
        with self._lock:  # Simulating atomic CAS
            current = self._value
            if current == expected:
                self._value = new_value
                return True
            return False
    
    def increment(self):
        """Atomically increment counter using CAS loop"""
        while True:
            current = self._value
            new_value = current + 1
            # Try to swap: if no one changed _value, succeed
            if self.compare_and_swap(current, new_value):
                return new_value
            # Otherwise, current changed; retry with new value
    
    def get(self):
        return self._value

# Usage
counter = AtomicCounter(10)
print(f"Value: {counter.increment()}")  # Prints: 11
examples.explanation: The increment method uses a CAS loop: read current value, compute new value, attempt CAS. If another thread modified the value, CAS fails and the loop retries with the updated value.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) amortized, O(n) worst case with high contention where n is number of retries

examples.title: Peterson's Algorithm with Hardware Assumptions
examples.description: Software solution showing required hardware assumptions for correctness
examples.explanation: This shows the hardware requirements for Peterson's algorithm to work correctly: atomic operations on shared variables, memory ordering guarantees, and cache coherence. Modern multiprocessors violate these assumptions, requiring hardware synchronization primitives.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) worst case entry, O(n) with n processes contending

examples.title: Lock-Free Stack using CAS
examples.description: Thread-safe stack implementation using Compare-And-Swap for push and pop operations
examples.explanation: The push and pop methods use CAS loops: read current top, prepare new top, attempt atomic update. If another thread modified the stack, CAS fails and the loop retries. This is lock-free because no thread ever blocks waiting.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) average case (amortized), O(n) worst case with high contention
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) per node, O(n) total for n elements in stack
